so_what_map.Rack Power Density: Why it matters: the per-rack power figure determines every component in the distribution chain — transformer, bus section, cable, PDU. A 5× density increase means a 5× infrastructure redesign.
so_what_map.Nameplate Rating vs Actual Draw: Why it matters: the gap between nameplate and actual draw represents stranded capacity that may be recapturable for higher-density racks — but only through measured redistribution, not assumption.
so_what_map.Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE): Why it matters: PUE determines both the energy cost overhead and EU Taxonomy alignment — at Clonshaugh's 1.50, nearly half the electricity bill goes to overhead, not computing.
so_what_map.Maximum Import Capacity (MIC): Why it matters: the MIC is the hard ceiling on total site power — exceeding it requires a grid connection upgrade that takes 12–24 months and costs €2M+, making it the longest lead time in any retrofit.
so_what_map.Power Distribution Unit (PDU): Why it matters: legacy 32A PDUs physically limit per-rack power delivery to approximately 7 kW — the first and cheapest bottleneck to identify and resolve in any density upgrade.
so_what_map.Hot Aisle Containment (HAC): Why it matters: HAC delivers the highest ROI of any single efficiency intervention — 30–40% cooling capacity improvement with payback often under 12 months.
so_what_map.Delta-T (ΔT): Why it matters: higher ΔT means more heat removed per unit of airflow — the efficiency lever that containment enables and that determines how far air cooling can be stretched.
so_what_map.Blanking Panel: Why it matters: at €3–8 per panel, blanking is the cheapest airflow intervention in any data centre — eliminating the most common cause of hotspots that artificially limit rack density.
so_what_map.Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD): Why it matters: CFD replaces guesswork with prediction — a €20K study prevents €200K of misplaced cooling infrastructure.
so_what_map.Cooling Redundancy (N+1): Why it matters: increasing density without reassessing cooling redundancy creates a hidden resilience risk — the same N+1 that worked at 8 kW provides less protection at 15 kW.
so_what_map.Volumetric Heat Capacity: Why it matters: air's 3,450:1 disadvantage versus water is the immutable physics that creates the air cooling ceiling — no engineering can overcome this ratio.
so_what_map.ASHRAE Class H1: Why it matters: H1's narrower temperature band (18–22°C) for AI/HPC hardware reduces operational flexibility and may limit free cooling hours — a hidden opex increase.
so_what_map.Fan Energy Penalty: Why it matters: fan power scales with the cube of airflow — at 30 kW/rack the fans themselves consume more energy than the original IT load, making air cooling economically irrational.
so_what_map.Acoustic Limit: Why it matters: noise regulations create a regulatory ceiling on air-cooled density that is lower than the thermal ceiling — even if the physics works at 35 kW, the noise may not comply.
so_what_map.Thermal Throttling: Why it matters: throttling means you pay for full power but receive 70–90% performance — the gap is invisible to the electricity bill but very visible to the AI workload.
so_what_map.Total Cost of Ownership (TCO): Why it matters: TCO reveals that liquid cooling is cheaper than air above 25 kW over any lease term longer than 3 years — the capex advantage of air disappears in year 2.
so_what_map.Direct-to-Chip Cooling (DLC): Why it matters: DLC is no longer experimental — with 22% adoption and 23% annual growth, it is the production standard for AI density, and choosing against it means choosing against the industry.
so_what_map.Coolant Distribution Unit (CDU): Why it matters: the CDU requires floor space and piping that legacy facilities do not have — planning its location is one of the earliest and most impactful retrofit design decisions.
so_what_map.Hybrid Cooling Architecture: Why it matters: every real retrofit operates hybrid (air + liquid) — planning for two cooling systems, two maintenance regimes, and two skill sets is essential and frequently underestimated.
so_what_map.Stranded Infrastructure Risk: Why it matters: investing in air cooling above 15 kW carries escalating risk of stranding because the industry trajectory is irrevocably toward liquid.
so_what_map.Cold Plate: Why it matters: cold plate quality directly determines GPU junction temperature and therefore performance — the component-level quality of the cooling determines the compute output.
so_what_map.Manifold: Why it matters: manifold design determines whether individual servers can be maintained without disrupting adjacent equipment — a critical operational capability at production scale.
so_what_map.Secondary Cooling Loop: Why it matters: the secondary loop volume determines leak consequence — a system holding 5,000 litres of coolant has a materially different risk profile than one holding 500 litres.
so_what_map.Coolant Selection: Why it matters: coolant choice ripples through the entire system — dielectric fluids require 2–3× the flow rate of water-glycol, meaning larger pipes, larger pumps, and larger CDUs.
so_what_map.Leak Detection System: Why it matters: leak detection is the safety system that enables liquid cooling in a live electrical environment — operating DLC without it is operating without a fire alarm.
so_what_map.GB300 NVL72 Rack Power Envelope: Why it matters: at 135 kW typical per rack, a single GB300 draws 17× a legacy rack — deploying even 10 racks consumes 27% of Clonshaugh's entire grid connection.
so_what_map.Power Sloshing: Why it matters: power sloshing means electrical distribution must handle 155 kW peak transients even though the average is 135 kW — sizing for average creates circuit protection failures.
so_what_map.50V DC Internal Busbar: Why it matters: the 50V busbar at 2,700A is at its engineering limit — the imminent transition to 800V DC (Rubin, 2027) will change how power is delivered to every rack.
so_what_map.Rack Weight (Liquid-Cooled GPU): Why it matters: at 3,000–3,500 lbs, a GB300 rack exceeds standard 150 psf floor ratings by a factor of 13 — structural assessment is mandatory before placement.
so_what_map.1,400W Per GPU: Why it matters: GPU power is doubling every 2 years — today's 135 kW rack becomes 200+ kW within one hardware generation, making future-proofing a present-day requirement.
so_what_map.Bus Section Rating: Why it matters: the bus section is the most common binding constraint in AI retrofit distribution — it determines the maximum power that can flow from transformer to distribution boards.
so_what_map.Protection Coordination (Discrimination): Why it matters: a discrimination failure at AI density means a single rack fault cascades into a zone-wide outage — the financial exposure is orders of magnitude larger than at legacy density.
so_what_map.Cable Sizing (Current-Carrying Capacity): Why it matters: AI-density cables are 25–30× heavier per circuit than legacy — existing cable trays, floor voids, and containment are almost certainly insufficient.
so_what_map.Busbar Trunking System: Why it matters: at AI density, busbar trunking becomes cost-competitive with cable because cable cross-sections make traditional cable management physically impractical.
so_what_map.Voltage Drop: Why it matters: voltage drop constrains the maximum distance between sub-distribution and rack — at AI density, the electrical room may need to be inside the hall, consuming rack positions.
so_what_map.I²R Loss: Why it matters: at AI density, distribution losses can exceed 1% of total IT load — voltage optimisation is the primary lever, because doubling voltage reduces I²R losses by 75%.
so_what_map.415V Three-Phase (European Standard): Why it matters: 415V is what Ireland has and what every supplier supports — but it becomes increasingly inefficient at densities above 50 kW per rack.
so_what_map.480V Three-Phase (US Standard): Why it matters: 480V offers 27% lower distribution losses versus 415V — a meaningful saving at scale, but with non-standard procurement overhead in the Irish market.
so_what_map.800V DC Distribution: Why it matters: 800V DC eliminates 2–3 conversion stages and is the only viable approach at 400+ kW per rack — any retrofit designed today must plan pathways for this transition.
so_what_map.Conversion Efficiency Chain: Why it matters: the cumulative conversion efficiency determines how much of the electricity purchased from the grid actually reaches the GPU — the difference between 87% and 95% is €710K/year.
so_what_map.Retrofit NPV (Net Present Value): Why it matters: NPV shows that the retrofit is strongly value-creating at any density above 40 kW with fill rates above 35% — the investment case is robust.
so_what_map.Fill Rate Risk: Why it matters: fill rate is the single variable that can make a positive-NPV retrofit destroy value — phased deployment is the primary mitigation.
so_what_map.Phased Deployment: Why it matters: phasing trades a 10% cost premium for a 36% reduction in peak capital at risk — a rational trade for any multi-asset fund.
so_what_map.Opportunity Cost of Inaction: Why it matters: doing nothing costs €12M+ in revenue loss and asset devaluation over the lease — more than the most expensive retrofit scenario.
so_what_map.Density Scenario Selection: Why it matters: higher density has lower breakeven fill rates because revenue scales faster than infrastructure cost — the seemingly riskier option is actually more resilient.

L1.asset_management: AI tenants generate 5–17× the revenue per rack position but require a power chain that no legacy facility can deliver without investment.
L1.technology: Every component from transformer to PDU was sized for 8 kW — identify the binding constraint before designing the solution.
L1.technical: MIC headroom is misleading — measure utilisation at every stage of the distribution chain independently to find the actual bottleneck.
L1.compliance: PUE at 1.50 means 40% of electricity is overhead — any power chain upgrade that reduces overhead simultaneously improves Taxonomy trajectory.
L1.cost: A €15K power quality survey identifies the exact upgrade path before committing capital — the cheapest insurance against misdirected investment.

L2.asset_management: Containment in Hall A delivered 230% ROI in year one from combined energy savings and density-enabled lease revenue.
L2.technology: HAC deploys in 14 days with zero tenant downtime — it is the fastest path to moderate density improvement while liquid cooling is planned.
L2.technical: Post-containment ΔT improved from 11°C to 19°C — consistent with ASHRAE TC 9.9 predictions for effective hot aisle containment.
L2.compliance: CRAH setpoint increase from 15°C to 23°C after containment reduced cooling energy by 14%, improving PUE from 1.50 toward 1.42.
L2.cost: Total containment project: €200K for 200 positions, with €95K/year energy savings and €320K/year additional lease revenue — payback under 5 months.

L3.asset_management: At 30 kW air-cooled, fan energy costs nearly €20K/rack/year — liquid cooling at the same density costs €6–8K/rack/year.
L3.technology: The practical air ceiling in Hall B with 3.2m ceiling height is approximately 25 kW — not the theoretical 41 kW.
L3.technical: Airflow at 41 kW per rack requires 1.7 m³/s with fan power approaching 20 kW — the cooling system would consume as much energy as the IT load.
L3.compliance: Air cooling at 40 kW would push the AI zone PUE to approximately 1.45 — failing EU Taxonomy alignment and creating a SFDR reporting liability.
L3.cost: The economic crossover where liquid cooling operating cost undercuts air cooling occurs at approximately 25 kW/rack — well below the physics ceiling.

L4.asset_management: DLC capex premium over air is approximately €660K for 50 racks — recovered in under 2 years from energy savings alone, before counting future-proofing value.
L4.technology: CDU lead time is 12–16 weeks — place orders within 4 months or risk missing the hyperscaler prospect's deployment timeline.
L4.technical: Coolant flow for 50 racks at 40 kW: 52.6 l/s through DN80/DN100 piping at under 2 m/s to prevent erosion.
L4.compliance: DLC delivers zone PUE of approximately 1.10–1.15 versus air at 1.35–1.50 — the difference between Taxonomy-aligned and non-aligned.
L4.cost: DLC capex premium of €660K has a simple payback under 2 years — the decision should be framed as TCO over the lease, not capex on day one.

L5.asset_management: 50-rack DLC zone at 100 kW: capex approximately €4–6M, revenue approximately €4.5–6M/year — payback under 12 months if racks are filled.
L5.technology: The DLC system connects to existing chilled water — no new chillers required if the current plant has capacity for the additional heat rejection.
L5.technical: Total secondary loop volume: approximately 4,000 litres for 50 racks — leak detection, containment, and drainage must be designed from the outset.
L5.compliance: The DLC zone at 1.08–1.12 PUE would bring facility-wide blended PUE below 1.30 for the first time, crossing the Taxonomy threshold.
L5.cost: DLC-specific infrastructure: approximately €2.43M for 50 racks at 100 kW — separate from power distribution and grid upgrades.

L6.asset_management: Ten GB300 racks generate €2.4–3.2M/year from 10 positions that previously earned €172K — a 14–19× revenue multiplier per rack position.
L6.technology: 800mm × 1200mm GB300 cabinets do not fit existing 600mm × 1000mm row spacing — aisle widths need complete re-evaluation.
L6.technical: Structural loading at 3,200 lbs per GB300 rack creates 2,000 psf point load — 13× the typical 150 psf raised floor rating, requiring slab-to-slab mounting.
L6.compliance: GB300 deployment adds 1.35 MW IT load, triggering CRU/2025236 renewable obligation for 80% of the additional capacity.
L6.cost: GB300-specific increment above base DLC: approximately €980K–1.05M for 10 racks — the premium for moving from 100 kW to 135 kW density.

L7.asset_management: Power distribution redesign: €800K–1.5M, approximately 20–30% of total retrofit budget — it appears to duplicate existing infrastructure but the existing infrastructure cannot serve AI density.
L7.technology: Busbar trunking provides plug-and-play flexibility for rack redeployment during 2–3 year GPU hardware refresh cycles — a hidden operational benefit.
L7.technical: Bus section at 73% and PDUs at near-maximum are the two binding constraints in Hall B — both must be addressed simultaneously.
L7.compliance: All electrical redesign requires REC certification under IS 10101:2020+AC2:2025 — the AC2:2025 corrigendum must be applied.
L7.cost: Total power distribution redesign: €500K–730K at 40 kW density, scaling to €800K–1.2M at GB300 density.

L8.asset_management: 415V with 800V DC future-proofing costs €120K — insuring against a €1.5–2.5M re-wire that has over 65% probability within 10 years.
L8.technology: Deploy 415V now with three provisions: oversized trays, reserved electrical room space, and pre-installed empty conduits for 800V DC.
L8.technical: I²R loss at 415V: 1.34 kW per rack at 135 kW density — approximately 1% of IT load wasted as heat in conductors.
L8.compliance: 480V installation in Ireland requires specialist IS 10101 engineering assessment — the standard is written for 230/400V nominal.
L8.cost: 800V DC pathway future-proofing: €120K. Cost of not future-proofing if 800V DC is needed: €1.5–2.5M. Expected value ratio: 10:1 in favour.

L9.asset_management: At 100 kW with 80% fill, NPV is €28M on €8M capex — a 3.5× return. Phase to limit peak capital at risk to €5M.
L9.technology: Build 100 kW infrastructure with 135 kW-capable pathways — the €800K premium eliminates a second infrastructure project when density increases.
L9.technical: All three density scenarios trigger MIC upgrade — grid connection is the critical path regardless of density choice.
L9.compliance: Doing nothing does not avoid regulatory obligations — it just reports worse numbers under EED, Taxonomy, and CRU/2025236.
L9.cost: Opportunity cost of inaction: €12M+ over the lease in lost revenue and asset devaluation — exceeding any retrofit scenario by a factor of 2.

glossary.definition: Electrical power consumed by IT equipment within a single rack, measured in kW/rack.
The contractual maximum power a site may draw from the grid, specified in the ESB Networks connection agreement.
Ratio of total facility energy to IT equipment energy, per ISO/IEC 30134-2:2026.
The copper or aluminium conductors within a switchboard that carry the main current between incoming and outgoing circuits.
A device distributing electrical power to IT equipment within a rack, providing metering and circuit protection.
Physical enclosure around the hot exhaust side of rack rows to prevent hot air recirculation.
Temperature difference between rack inlet and exhaust air, indicating heat removal effectiveness.
Numerical simulation of airflow and temperature distribution within a data centre space.
ASHRAE TC 9.9 thermal class for high-density computing, recommended inlet 18–22°C.
Thermal energy absorbed per unit volume per degree of temperature rise (kJ/m³·K).
Relationships governing fan performance: power scales with the cube of flow rate.
Automatic reduction in processor speed when component temperatures exceed design limits.
Complete lifecycle cost including capex, opex, energy, maintenance, and end-of-life.
Liquid cooling method using cold plates mounted directly on heat-generating components.
Heat exchanger managing the secondary loop between facility cooling and liquid-cooled IT equipment.
Thermally conductive block with internal micro-channels, mounted on GPUs/CPUs for direct heat absorption.
Piping distribution assembly within/adjacent to a rack, splitting coolant supply to individual servers.
Closed piping circuit between CDU and rack manifolds, separated from facility primary loop.
Combined air and liquid cooling within a single facility or rack (e.g., GB300: 90% liquid, 10% air).
Capital invested in infrastructure that becomes economically unrecoverable due to technology change.
NVIDIA rack-scale AI platform: 72 Blackwell Ultra GPUs, 132–142 kW TDP, 155 kW peak, fully liquid-cooled.
Dynamic power redistribution between compute trays in a GB300 rack based on workload phase.
Internal rack power distribution at 50V DC in GB300, carrying 2,700A — at current engineering limits.
Emerging architecture delivering medium-voltage DC to the rack, demonstrated for NVIDIA Rubin (2027).
Arrangement of protective devices so that a fault is cleared by the nearest device without cascading trips.
Irish National Rules for Electrical Installations — the mandatory standard for all LV electrical work.
European standard for low-voltage switchgear and controlgear assemblies.
European standard series for data centre facilities and infrastructures.
Power dissipated as heat in conductors, proportional to current squared times resistance.
Reduction in voltage along a conductor due to current flow through its resistance.
Prefabricated modular power distribution system using rigid conductors in sealed housing.
Present value of future cash flows minus initial investment, discounted at the required rate of return.
Percentage of available rack positions occupied by revenue-generating tenants.
Delivering infrastructure in sequential phases to reduce peak capital exposure.
Value lost by not investing — measured in tenant attrition, asset devaluation, and competitive decline.
CRU Large Energy User Connection Policy (Dec 2025) — 80% renewable sourcing obligation.
Data centre PUE threshold for Taxonomy alignment under Delegated Act 2021/2139 §8.1.
Recast Energy Efficiency Directive — mandatory annual energy reporting for DCs ≥500 kW IT.
Sustainable Finance Disclosure Regulation — classifies funds (Article 6/8/9) based on sustainability.
The year a facility's carbon intensity exceeds its CRREM decarbonisation pathway (LBE term — CRREM does not publish a DC-specific pathway).
